I ran across this self-proclaimed copper bull on my feed, and the guy was literally wishing a death curse on the entire tech complex—EVs, AI, the whole nine yards.

Honestly, I was completely dumbfounded. I’ve been sitting on a massive book of copper equities for years now, but if you’re long Dr. Copper, you should be aggressively praying that Jensen Huang and Sam Altman don't get locked up and AI data centers don't spontaneously combust.

Legacy copper demand has been flatlining for a minute now. We've all heard the "China property sector is toast" macro tape until we're blue in the face. Granted this cycle is heavily supply-driven, but the demand side is anchored to the high tech narrative.

It's the energy transition and data centers that are holding up the structural demand thesis. This frontier demand is ramping on a hockey-stick trajectory; EVs were the headline act early on, but grid and transmission capex are rapidly becoming the real heavy lifters.

Backstopped by the AI revolution and this insatiable hunger for power, global grid capex is compounding exponentially, and copper is running the textbook picks and shovels play.

Also structurally speaking, an EV gobbles up almost 3x the copper intensity of a legacy ICE vehicle, meaning EVs already occupy a massive chunk of the demand pie chart.

To top it off AI data centers are more than double the copper-intensity of a vanilla crypto mining warehouse. The data center demand vector is moving in a straight line up and right.

And yet, some guys still want Tesla and Nvidia to go to zero? Give me a break. We need these tech nerds to keep printing.

#copper $hbm

Russia shutting down diesel exports feels like a forced hand. Doesn't matter the jurisdiction—the moment pump prices heat up, the retail public starts grabbing pitchforks.

Putin's gotta be catching a tilt right about now. Solid work Zelenskyy!

Earlier today, I gave this comment on #gold to @business: 
Considering what was thrown at gold yesterday - surging oil and fuel prices, hawkish signals from the FOMC minutes, rising yields and traders once again entertaining the possibility of a rate hike - it has actually held up remarkably well. The metal found support ahead of USD 4,050 and is now bouncing as the dollar and yields trade softer.
In fact, the dollar has traded broadly unchanged over the past two days, potentially signalling some buying fatigue among traders already holding a very elevated speculative long position. Overall, the price action over the past 24 hours strengthens my belief that gold has moved from capitulation to consolidation.
That view could obviously be challenged if oil prices continue to rise. However, given the recent weakness in US jobs data, I do not think the FOMC will automatically reach for the rate-hike button, even if higher energy prices increase the risk of inflation remaining elevated for longer.

EIA crude and fuel stocks report:

US crude inventories rose for the first time in 11 weeks, increasing by 3 million barrels as exports slowed to 3.3 million barrels per day, the lowest since November. A further 6.2 million barrels were drawn from the SPR, while production rose to 13.86 million barrels per day, close to last year’s record high.

Distillate inventories, including diesel, slumped by 5 million barrels to a four-year low, while a 1.9 million-barrel drop in gasoline stocks pushed inventories to their lowest seasonal level since 2012.

US refined-product exports jumped to a record 8.7 million barrels per day, lifting total oil and fuel exports, including crude, to 12 million barrels per day. US gasoline demand on a four-week average basis, held just below 9 million barrels per day and lowest since the Fourth of July holiday in 2022

Federal Reserve meeting minutes: Some officials support raising interest rates in June, but disagreements are actually "waiting for data" In the early hours of Thursday morning Beijing time, the Federal Reserve released the minutes of the first interest rate meeting chaired by new chairman Kevin Warsh.

Just in: U.S. stocks before the market opens: Three major stock index futures are mixed, technology stocks are strong before the market opens, and the collapse of the US-Iran ceasefire intensifies concerns about the Fed turning hawkish.

Before the market opened on Thursday, U.S. stock index futures were mixed, with most major European indexes rising. As of press time, Nasdaq 100 futures were up 0.89%, Dow futures were down 0.02%, and S&P 500 futures were up 0.26%. In terms of commodities, Brent crude oil rose 0.50% to US$78.41/barrel; WTI crude oil rose 0.23% to US$73.26/barrel.

Spot gold rose 0.86% to $4,112.66 an ounce. Spot silver rose 1.39% to $59.13. In terms of individual stocks, most star technology stocks were higher before the market opened. Micron Technology rose by more than 5%, Intel rose by more than 4%, and Meta fell by more than 2%.

Popular Chinese concept stocks weakened slightly, with Pinduoduo falling 2% and Alibaba rising 0.48%. Storage concepts strengthened before the opening, with Huirong Technology rising by 6%, Micron Technology and Western Digital rising by more than 5%. Optical communications stocks collectively rose, with Marvell Technology and Corning rising more than 5%.

Semiconductor equipment and materials rose before the market opened. Lam Group rose more than 5%, Applied Materials rose nearly 5%, and ASML rose more than 2%. AI application software stocks fell before the market opened, with Palantir falling 2%, ServiceNow falling nearly 4%, and Salesforce falling more than 4%.

According to reports, Starbucks has launched "self-developed software" to replace it, and Microsoft and IBM are facing new challenges. The International Monetary Fund (IMF) pointed out in its latest economic outlook report that the actual damage caused by the U.S.-Iran conflict to the U.S.

and global economies is less than what the market previously predicted. However, this geopolitical turmoil will generate a new round of sustained inflationary pressure, and the problem of rising prices will not completely subside in the short term.

The IMF said that after the United States and Iran reached a fragile ceasefire, international oil prices fell faster than market expectations, but the inflationary damage caused by the conflict has already been formed, and the repair process will take a long time. Even if the U.S.-Iran war comes to an end, U.S.

inflation will have to wait until the end of 2027 to fall back to the Fed's 2% policy target range, making it difficult to get close to this standard in the short term.

The second day of the "end" of the ceasefire: The US military expands the scope of its strikes, Iran's retaliation spreads to Qatar bases, Trump talks while fighting The conflict between the United States and Iran entered its second day after Trump announced the "end" of the ceasefire agreement, and the fighting escalated rapidly.

On July 9, according to Xinhua News Agency, the U.S.

Central Command posted on social media that it had completed another round of strikes against Iran, involving about 90 Iranian military targets, including air defense systems, coastal surveillance facilities, missile and drone storage facilities, and military logistics infrastructure in Iran's coastal areas.

According to Xinhua News Agency, the Islamic Republic of Iran Radio and Television reported on the 9th that the Iranian military’s public relations department issued a statement saying that not long ago, the Iranian military used drones to launch strikes on multiple US military targets in the Middle East in response to the US military’s aggression against Iran.

The statement said that the Iranian military used a large number of attack drones of various types to attack the US military "Patriot" air defense system in Kuwait, the US military satellite antenna in Qatar, and the US military fuel storage facilities in Bahrain. Previously, Trump sent conflicting signals during the NATO summit.

According to Xinhua News Agency, U.S.

President Trump said at the NATO summit on the 8th that he believed the U.S.-Iran Memorandum of Understanding "has ended." Subsequently, Trump said in the Turkish capital Ankara on Wednesday that the current escalation of the situation between the United States and Iran would soon subside and that he did not believe that a war with Iran would break out again.

The "fight and talk" pattern makes the prospect of conflict confusing, and international oil prices fluctuate violently. Brent oil briefly broke through the US$80 mark and then gave up its gains. Oil shipments across the key strait were almost blocked, and the global energy market faced uncertainty again.

HSBC lowers gold target price as hawkish Fed and strong US dollar continue to put pressure HSBC lowered its average gold price forecast for 2026 and 2027 on Thursday, citing a shift in market expectations for U.S. monetary policy to be hawkish and a strengthening U.S. dollar, which has continued to put pressure on the precious metals market.

It is reported that HSBC lowered its average gold price forecast in 2026 to US$4,560 per ounce from the previous US$4,864 per ounce, and lowered its average price forecast in 2027 from US$5,000 to US$4,925.

The bank predicts that the price of gold will fluctuate in the range of US$3,800 to US$4,700 per ounce for the remainder of 2026, with the price at the end of the year being approximately US$4,750; the price of gold is expected to reach US$5,025 per ounce by the end of 2027.

Previously, conflicts in the Middle East triggered concerns about inflation and prompted market expectations that the Federal Reserve would adopt a more hawkish monetary policy stance. The U.S.

military said it launched another air strike on Iran on Wednesday, and Iran subsequently launched retaliatory attacks on Kuwait and Bahrain, further escalating tensions in the Middle East. However, U.S.

President Trump said that Iran has proactively contacted the United States to seek an agreement, which alleviated some of the market's concerns about the further deterioration of the situation in the Middle East. HSBC said: "The change in the market's view of U.S. monetary policy and the impact of this change on the U.S.

dollar is one of the core reasons why gold has suffered further selling and prices have continued to fall." Goldman Sachs: The Fed's baseline scenario for the year is still on hold, and the path of inflation will determine whether to raise or cut interest rates.

Goldman Sachs pointed out in its latest research report that the minutes of the Federal Reserve’s June meeting showed that all FOMC participants supported maintaining the federal funds rate target range at 3.5% to 3.75%, but a few participants believed that there was reason to raise interest rates at that time.

The minutes showed that the Fed's policy path is still highly dependent on inflation trends, rather than a unidirectional shift to easing or tightening. The minutes of the meeting showed that participants generally believed that U.S.

inflation has risen further and is significantly higher than the 2% target, due to tariffs, supply chain disruptions, and AI investment demand.

"Several" participants pointed out that price pressures have become broader, involving areas such as transportation, air tickets, petrochemicals and agricultural inputs; service sector inflation remains high after excluding housing.

Most participants also believed that AI-related investment demand pushes the economy to grow faster than potential, which may make inflation more sustainable. However, the labor market was seen as remaining stable, with many participants saying it was not currently a source of inflationary pressures.

Economic activity is still expanding at a steady pace, mainly supported by AI-related business investment; higher stock prices and previous tax rebates have also boosted consumption, especially spending by high-income households. Goldman Sachs predicts that the U.S.

core PCE growth rate will drop from the current 3.4% to 3.0% in December 2026, and the core CPI will drop from the current 2.9% to 2.6%. The core CPI month-on-month readings in the coming months are expected to be moderate.

The bank believes that although there are still risks of interest rate hikes this year, the baseline scenario is still for the Federal Reserve to maintain policy interest rates unchanged in 2026.

Meta’s self-developed AI chip “Iris” will be mass-produced as soon as September, and its computing power is expected to double to 14 gigawatts in 2027 Meta is accelerating its self-research strategy for chips in order to reduce its dependence on external suppliers such as Nvidia.

On July 9, according to an internal memo seen by Reuters, Meta plans to start mass production of its self-developed AI chip code-named "Iris" as soon as September this year, and plans to maintain an iterative rhythm of launching a new generation of chips approximately every six months until 2027.

At the same time, the company aims to double the total data center computing capacity to 14 gigawatts (GW) in 2027. This means that Meta is accelerating to get rid of its high dependence on Nvidia GPUs, reducing AI infrastructure costs through self-developed chips, and enhancing computing power autonomy.

The memorandum shows that the "Iris" chip completed key tests in just six weeks, and no major problems were found. The progress is significantly faster than the pace of similar chips in the industry that usually takes several months to verify, showing that Meta's self-research project has made a substantial breakthrough. As of press time, Meta's U.S.

stocks fell more than 2.5% before the market opened.

PepsiCo's Q2 revenue exceeds expectations, weak performance in North America drags down earnings The financial report shows that PepsiCo's Q2 net revenue increased 6.4% year-on-year to US$24.18 billion, which was better than the market expectation of US$23.95 billion; operating profit was US$4.02 billion, slightly lower than the US$4.06 billion expected by the market; core earnings per share was US$2.20, slightly better than the US$2.19 expected by the market.

Although revenue continued to grow, profit performance was slightly lacking. The core drag on performance comes from the North American market. The company's management pointed out that sales in the U.S. food and beverage sector have slowed, inflation is still squeezing household budgets, and consumers have to cut daily spending.

Specifically, snack sales in North America were basically flat, while beverage sales fell by 4%. In contrast to North America, overseas markets continue to provide support - the demand for snacks and beverages in the international market is relatively strong, driving overall snack sales to increase by 3% and beverage sales to increase by 2%.

This partially helped offset weakness in North America. In addition, the company still forecasts endogenous revenue growth of 2% to 4% for the full year of 2026, and the median forecast range of 3% is better than the market expectation of 2.76%.

Ahead of ASML’s Q2 results, Xiaomo shares are as high as $2,200: short-term performance is not the real test, and the outlook for 2027 is the key to revaluation Lithography giant ASML will announce its second quarter 2026 results on July 15.

Due to recent market concerns about the sustainability of artificial intelligence (AI) capital expenditures, ASML's stock price has been relatively sluggish - its US stock price has fallen by more than 11% since July.

In a recently released research report, JPMorgan Chase previewed ASML's second-quarter performance and pointed out that the company needs to release signals of capacity expansion and strong demand in 2027 and beyond before the stock price is expected to achieve a breakthrough.

Regarding ASML's second-quarter performance, JPMorgan Chase expects the company's second-quarter revenue to reach 8.7 billion euros (a year-on-year increase of 13.1% and a month-on-month decrease of 0.8%), which is 1.4% lower than the market consensus forecast, but in line with the mid-point of the company's previously given guidance range of 8.4-9 billion euros.

In terms of profitability, JPMorgan Chase estimates that the company’s gross profit margin in the second quarter will be 51.7%, which is within the company’s guidance range (51%-52%) and slightly lower than the market consensus of 52%; second quarter earnings per share are expected to be 6.67 euros, 2.3% lower than the market consensus expectation.

ASML previously predicted that revenue in 2026 will increase by 16.3% year-on-year to approximately 38 billion euros (based on the mid-range value). Currently, the market's consensus expectation for ASML's revenue growth in 2026 is 19.8%, which is higher than the mid-point of the guidance range given by the company, but still within the guidance range.

JPMorgan Chase expects ASML to raise its 2026 performance guidance due to stronger-than-expected shipments of DUV lithography equipment.

Although JPMorgan Chase's forecast for ASML's second-quarter performance is lower than the market consensus, its analysis shows that short-term performance may not be of much significance to the stock price, and the real focus of the market will be 2027.

The bank believes that ASML's growth in 2027 is expected to be significantly higher than the overall growth of the global wafer fab equipment (WFE) market.

This cannot be achieved in 2026 because customer orders started late (starting in December 2025), resulting in the supply chain having no time to increase production capacity and therefore unable to deliver more EUV equipment in 2026.

JPMorgan Chase estimates ASML’s earnings per share in 2027 and 2028 to be 54.4 euros and 64.4 euros respectively, which are 26.5% and 23.8% higher than current market expectations respectively. (

Just in: Pre-market: Nasdaq futures rose 0.64%, the market is betting that the United States and Iran will not go into full-scale war.

Oil price gains were brought under control on Thursday (July 9) after the United States ended its second consecutive day of strikes against Iran, and stock markets rebounded from a brief period of geopolitical risk aversion. As of press time, Dow futures fell 0.13%, S&P 500 futures rose 0.15%, and Nasdaq futures rose 0.64%.

Europe's Stoxx 600 rebounded from its biggest one-day selloff since March. In the London market, Disappointing results from a trial of a heart disease drug sent its shares down 9.4%. Shares of chipmakers in Asia, Europe and the United States rose after strong demand for SK Hynix's American depositary receipt issuance.

After days of wild swings, chipmakers showed signs of repair after the bell. Technology rose 3.4%, The conflict between the United States and Iran escalates, and the market reassesses risks Renewed tensions are forcing stock investors to reassess previously optimistic expectations.

The market had previously believed that a final resolution of the U.S.-Iran conflict could support risk assets. On Wednesday, the S&P 500 and The industrial average closed lower, while The composite index managed to end slightly higher. The U.S.

military said on Wednesday it had launched a new round of strikes against Iran to ensure the Strait of Hormuz remains open to shipping. Iran responded by attacking Kuwait and Bahrain, deepening the confrontation between the two sides and putting the already fragile ceasefire efforts at risk of being derailed. The escalation came hours after U.S.

President Donald Trump said he believed the temporary ceasefire with Iran was "over." Market sentiment remained calm even as violence escalated in the Middle East and threatened U.S. efforts to reach a permanent peace deal with Iran.

Mark Haefele, chief investment officer of Global Wealth Management, said: "We have always believed that the road to a lasting peace agreement may be bumpy, and that periodic increases in tensions may trigger market volatility.

However, we also believe that both sides still have incentives to keep the Strait of Hormuz open." Oil prices fall, but supply risks remain Brent crude fell 0.5% to $77.60 a barrel. The previous trading day, Brent crude oil recorded its largest single-day gain since May. Supply concerns are also driven by Russia, especially in middle distillates.

Due to continued drone attacks on oil refining infrastructure in Ukraine and fuel shortages in Russia, Russia announced a ban on diesel exports until the end of July.

Group analysts said: "The price action over the past few days clearly shows that the market has been too relaxed about the risks surrounding this agreement and is too optimistic that regional supply can rebound quickly." Although the day's drop in crude oil prices helped stabilize market sentiment, investors remain wary: If Middle East energy transportation routes are disrupted for a long time, it may bring new inflation risks.

Traders said that while the tensions reflected the fragile nature of the ceasefire, neither government wanted a full-scale return to war and the two sides were likely to eventually return to the negotiating table. Geoff Yu, senior macro strategist at BNY, said: "This is the new status quo; it's an uneasy balance, but a balance nonetheless.

You just have to factor volatility into your asset allocation." Bonds stop falling and stabilize, yields fall Global bonds edged higher as two days of oil-driven bond selloff came to an end. The U.S. 2-year Treasury yield fell 2 basis points to 4.20%.

"However, the market appears to have stabilized for now, with Brent crude hovering around $79 a barrel and U.S. yields and German Bund futures trading sideways in overnight trading in Asia," said Danske Bank analyst August Hyldgaard.

Euro zone government bond and gilt yields fell as oil prices turned lower, reversing some of the previous session's sharp gains. The yield on 10-year German government bonds fell 1.8 basis points to last reported at 3.067%; the yield on 10-year British government bonds fell 3.1 basis points to 4.935%.

Disagreement emerges in Fed minutes, market awaits inflation data The dollar was little changed. Minutes from the Federal Reserve's latest meeting showed policymakers were divided on the future path of interest rates.

Minutes of the Federal Reserve's June 17 meeting released on Wednesday showed that some committee members believed there were reasons to raise interest rates. Traders will now await U.S. inflation data next week and testimony from Federal Reserve Chairman Josh Warsh to lawmakers for further judgment on the path of interest rates.

Jefferies economist Mohit Kumar said in a report that compared with the "hawkish interpretation" triggered by the June press conference, the minutes were more balanced. The June press conference had boosted market expectations for interest rate hikes.

He said many Fed policymakers believe price pressures will ease in the medium term and policy rates can remain stable or gradually lower. "Our view remains that as long as there is no significant spike in oil prices, we do not expect the Fed to raise rates this year and still expect the next move to be a rate cut, possibly next year," Kumar said.

Oil prices remain an important variable for Fed hawks "While policymakers are likely to remain hawkish for a while longer, the rhetoric should start to soften once they become more confident that the effects of second-round inflation will be limited," Haefele said.

Traders now expect the Fed to raise interest rates at least once before the end of the year, according to LSEG data. New York Fed President John Williams will also participate in a moderated discussion later Thursday.

"Our bias is that higher energy prices will provide justification for a hawkish Fed and provide support if the dollar pulls back, especially against lower-yielding currencies," wrote Chris Turner, ING's head of FX strategy. Nearly 70% of U.S. technology stocks fell into bear market territory! AI faith faces a big test: profit-taking or cycle peak?

The AI star stocks that have led the market in the past few months are now collectively on fire - as many as 69% of the constituent stocks in the S&P 500 information technology sector have fallen more than 20% from their 52-week high and entered a bear market.

But while the sell-off is significant, some analysts don't think it necessarily means a trend reversal. They believe that it is a natural process for investors to take profits after the previous sharp rise.

Some analysts pointed out that in the past few quarters, technology stocks usually came under pressure within a month after the release of earnings reports, and then rebounded before the release of the next earnings report. In a research report this week, it was stated that the AI-driven technology upward cycle has not yet shown a peak signal.

The report believes that the supply of semiconductors and electronic components has not yet exceeded demand, and technological evolution shows no signs of slowing down. The Wall Street giant views the recent market correction as a healthy recovery after a rapid rise in stock prices, rather than a trend reversal in tech stocks.

Goldman Sachs: The Fed's baseline scenario for the year is still to hold on to the inflation path and decide whether to raise or cut interest rates.

Goldman Sachs pointed out in its latest research report that the minutes of the Federal Reserve’s June meeting showed that all FOMC participants supported maintaining the federal funds rate target range at 3.5% to 3.75%, but a few participants believed that there was reason to raise interest rates at that time.

The minutes showed that the Fed's policy path is still highly dependent on inflation trends, rather than a unidirectional shift to easing or tightening. The research report stated that most participants discussed different macro scenarios.

If inflationary pressures subside soon and begin to return to near the 2% target, almost all relevant participants believe that keeping interest rates unchanged or eventually cutting interest rates may be appropriate options.

On the contrary, if inflation continues to be high due to AI-related demand, conflict in the Middle East, or the impact of tariffs, almost all relevant participants believe that further tightening of policy may be necessary. The minutes of the meeting showed that participants generally believed that U.S.

inflation has risen further and is significantly higher than the 2% target, due to tariffs, supply chain disruptions, and AI investment demand.

"Several" participants pointed out that price pressures have become broader, involving areas such as transportation, air tickets, petrochemicals and agricultural inputs; service sector inflation remains high after excluding housing.

Most participants also believed that AI-related investment demand pushes the economy to grow faster than potential, which may make inflation more sustainable. However, the labor market was seen as remaining stable, with many participants saying it was not currently a source of inflationary pressures.

Economic activity is still expanding at a steady pace, mainly supported by AI-related business investment; higher stock prices and previous tax rebates have also boosted consumption, especially spending by high-income households. Goldman Sachs predicts that the U.S.

core PCE growth rate will drop from the current 3.4% to 3.0% in December 2026, and the core CPI will drop from the current 2.9% to 2.6%. The core CPI month-on-month readings in the coming months are expected to be moderate.

The bank believes that although there are still risks of interest rate hikes this year, the baseline scenario is still for the Federal Reserve to maintain policy interest rates unchanged in 2026.

Goldman Sachs: Food and energy security policies may increase agricultural product price volatility Goldman Sachs pointed out in its latest research report that global agricultural supply is highly concentrated, with the top three exporters of key varieties such as corn, soybeans, rice, sugar and palm oil accounting for 60% to 90% of global trade volume, making the agricultural product market more vulnerable to local weather, geopolitical and policy shocks.

The bank believes that what really amplifies price fluctuations is not just the decline in production, but the protectionist measures taken by exporting countries to ensure domestic food and energy security.

Once there is a supply disturbance, or even just a disturbance in expectations, policies such as export restrictions, mandatory increases in biofuel blending ratios, and inventory hoarding may reduce exportable supply, reduce liquidity in the global trade market, and amplify price reactions.

Goldman Sachs mentioned that India accounts for about 40% of global rice exports. It banned the export of non-Basmati rice in 2023 due to concerns about El Niño. Although India's output ultimately remained resilient that year, rice prices in Asia rose significantly as a result.

Similarly, India recently restricted sugar exports due to the risk of a potential "super" El Niño. Energy security could also squeeze food supplies. From 2016 to 2026, Indonesia increased the blending ratio of biodiesel from 20% to 40% to 50%.

However, its palm oil production has limited growth due to land and yield constraints, resulting in a decline in exportable palm oil balances and pushing up the bottom of vegetable oil prices.

Goldman Sachs believes that agricultural product prices face three major upward risks in the future: El Niño has emerged and has a 63% probability of developing into a "super" El Niño; higher energy prices in the first half of 2026 may prompt more countries to increase their biofuel requirements; and if the Strait of Hormuz is disturbed again, it will affect the nitrogen fertilizer purchasing season in the third quarter.

The bank said that although protectionist policies are intended to enhance domestic resilience, they may exacerbate fragmentation and price volatility in global agricultural markets. The U.S. is a net exporter of petroleum products overall and produces a large share of its own oil domestically thanks to shale production.

Oil is a fungible global commodity. Its price is set on international markets (benchmarks like West Texas Intermediate or Brent Crude), not by where any single country sources its barrels. 
U.S. refineries buy crude at these global prices, and those costs flow through to gasoline at the pump.

Uran’s actions (including threats, attacks on shipping, and effective restrictions) sharply reduced oil flows out of the Persian Gulf. This created one of the largest oil supply shocks in recent history:

Seasonal effects: Refineries switch to a more expensive summer-blend gasoline to reduce evaporation in hot weather.
Demand: Summer driving season increases consumption.
“Rockets and feathers” effect: Gasoline prices tend to rise quickly when crude costs go up but fall more slowly when they ease.
Global competition: Higher world prices mean U.S. refiners and exporters face pressure from international buyers competing for available supply.

Increased U.S. oil output helps overall supply and supports exports, but it doesn’t isolate pump prices from global events. U.S. producers often sell at world prices, and the interconnected nature of the market means Middle East disruptions ripple everywhere.

Inventory and replacement cost pricing: 
Stations and wholesalers price based on what it will cost to replace their fuel, not just what they paid earlier. Rising costs force immediate hikes to avoid losses. Falling costs allow them to sell higher-priced inventory first before lowering prices.

The Head Fake That’s Working
Trump isn’t bluffing about Kharg Island. He’s playing it loud and hard while the bombs are already falling. The threat to seize Iran’s crown jewel oil terminal isn’t some idle campaign line — it’s a deliberate psychological and operational wedge. Loud, repeated, public. The goal isn’t just to scare Tehran. It’s to make them move.
Every time he says “we will be taking Kharg Island,” Iranian commanders have to decide whether to reinforce it, disperse more assets, fire more missiles, or move naval units. That movement creates signatures. More targets come out of their holes. That’s the real play right now — not a Marine landing tomorrow, but forcing the other side to react under pressure and reveal what they’ve been hiding.
On paper, seizing Kharg looks strategically clean: cut 90% of Iran’s crude export capacity, choke their revenue, reopen the Strait of Hormuz on American terms, and prove serious resolve. In reality, it’s a logistical and political meat grinder. The island sits 15 nautical miles off a hostile coast packed with Iranian missiles, drones, mines, and fast-attack craft. Holding it means defending against constant asymmetric attacks while trying to run or deny a massive oil export complex. This isn’t Iwo Jima with flags and victory. This is a potential tar baby that bleeds American lives and hands Iran a ready-made propaganda win for years.
Iran has been preparing. They drew down storage before the strikes hit. They’ve dispersed assets. They have mines, drone swarms, and proxy options ready to make any occupation painful and expensive. A full ground seizure would be high-cost, high-casualty, and strategically questionable for marginal gains once the initial shock wears off.
Trump knows this. That’s why the current pressure mix — real strikes on military targets plus the very public seizure threat — is smarter than a straight invasion. It keeps maximum leverage without immediately committing to the hardest option. Blockade plus selective, sustained strikes is sustainable. A boots-on-the-ground occupation of Kharg is not — unless Iran forces the issue by escalating badly.
Oil markets are already spooked. Any actual seizure or prolonged effective blockade will drive prices higher, and that pain lands on everyone, including American drivers and the global economy. China, Iran’s biggest customer, will not sit quietly. Regional players will adjust their own calculations. Energy is the weapon here, and both sides know how to use it.
This is classic great-power brinkmanship. Threats have power until they don’t. The head-fake works best when the other side can’t be sure whether it’s pure theater or the real thing. Right now, the uncertainty itself is doing work — flushing movement, forcing hard choices, and keeping Iran on the defensive while the US controls the tempo of the strikes.
The hard truth: We’ve seen versions of this movie before. Loud threats can achieve a lot. Actual occupation of a place like Kharg changes the game in ways that are very hard to control once the first boots hit the ground and the first missiles start flying back. The smart money is on sustained pressure and leverage, not a permanent American flag on that island.
That’s the play as it stands. The head game is active. More targets are moving. The question is whether Tehran blinks or doubles down.

Businesses and governments managed to keep energy prices from skyrocketing as much as feared during the Iran war by leaning into a “just-in-time” delivery system that harnesses innovations in digital and satellite technology and that reduces the need to stockpile barrels of oil.

Call it the “Amazon of oil,” said Jim Wicklund, a veteran oil analyst and managing director at the PPHB energy investment firm, comparing energy industry dynamics to the ecommerce giant’s famous mastery of inventory and logistics.

Even with President Trump declaring the Iran ceasefire “over” on Wednesday amid a fresh exchange of military strikes, the U.S. benchmark for crude prices still only spiked about 5% to $74 per barrel—way below the mid-May high of $112.

While energy traders may see the latest attacks and verbal barbs as dips along the negotiation rollercoaster, they’ve also been encouraged by the adaptability of global energy logistics, even amid the greatest global energy shock of the modern age when the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz temporarily cut off almost 20% of the world’s oil and liquefied natural gas supplies.

“When you go back to the 1970s when we had the oil shocks, you had no way of knowing what oil was where and what it was doing,” Wicklund told Fortune. “Today, I can hit my terminal and find every tanker full of oil on the ocean, who owns it, what’s in it, and who to call to get it diverted to me. So, inventories have not meant nearly as much to oil prices here in the last few years as they used to.

“The correlation between inventories and oil price has been dropping from a high correlation to almost no correlation today. I don’t need physical inventories like I used to. I can order immediately off the Amazon of oil and buy cargoes on the water,” he said.

Another saving grace for logistics was the Trump administration’s decision to temporarily waive the 106-year-old Jones Act, which requires cargo ships moving between U.S. ports to be U.S. built, flagged, and manned, reducing the number of vessels available to move crude oil and refined products between domestic ports.

The waiver allowed more ships, for instance, to move fuel from the U.S. Gulf Coast through the Panama Canal and up to California, which has dealt with newly shuttered refineries in recent months, to help alleviate shortfalls.

The China factor

T

While large commercial oil inventories haven’t meant as much as they once did, the national strategic reserves of the U.S. and especially China have proven pivotal. The other major reason oil prices didn’t rise as high as feared is because China built up its storage stockpiles to all-time highs and dramatically cut down on its imports after the war began.

Before the war began, China imported more than 11.5 million barrels per day. By June, China’s imports plunged below 7 million barrels daily, effectively lowering global oil demand by almost 5 million barrels per day. Although China doesn’t reveal many details, the U.S. government estimates China’s oil reserves had risen to about 1.4 billion barrels before the war began—the product of a yearslong emphasis on building up strategic stockpiles.

“China as a source of moderation on energy is something that is for sure new,” said Arjun Murti, energy macro and policy partner at the Veriten research and investment firm. “We did not guess or predict that China would reduce the oil imports by such a massive amount, which had such a huge impact.”

Likewise, the U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve is now at its lowest level since 1983, but it still holds more than 300 million barrels of crude—319 million barrels as of July 3—which is down from 415 million barrels at the beginning of the war.

And because Trump wants to keep fuel prices lower, there’s little chance the U.S. starts replenishing its strategic reserves before the midterm elections this year, analysts said. Trump has authorized the overall release of 172 million barrels over several months, so supplies could still dip much lower before they’re built back up maybe beginning next year.

The resiliency of the energy markets has quieted the “doomsdayers” who predicted record highs of $200 per barrel oil, Wicklund said.

While he never projected such high spikes, Wicklund was “surprised” oil prices have fallen as much as they have in late June and July.

“I’m starting to believe,” he said. “Instead of trying to figure out why the market is wrong, figure out why you’re wrong.”
